headline: Scottish Runner Josh Kerr Shatters 27-Year Mile Record
meta_description: Josh Kerr becomes fastest miler in history, breaking Hicham El Guerrouj's 27-year-old record with a time of 3:42.66 at the London Diamond League.
summary_short: Scottish runner Josh Kerr has broken the world mile record, clocking 3:42.66 at London's Diamond League—ending a 27-year reign by Moroccan legend Hicham El Guerrouj.
body:
Scottish runner Josh Kerr has claimed the fastest mile time in human history, breaking a record that stood for nearly three decades. Competing at the London Diamond League, the 28-year-old clocked 3:42.66—half a second faster than Moroccan legend Hicham El Guerrouj's 3:43.13, set in Rome in 1999 when Kerr was barely walking. The achievement caps an audacious year: Kerr had publicly announced his record-breaking ambitions at the start of 2024 and spent months in intense preparation, training in the hills of Albuquerque, New Mexico, under what he called "Project 222"—targeting a time of three minutes and forty-two seconds.

Kerr arrived at the starting line with credentials already impressive. An Olympic silver medalist holding the world record for the 1,500 meters, he had the pedigree to challenge El Guerrouj's legendary mark. On race day, pacemakers guided him through the first 1,000 meters before dropping away, leaving Kerr to sprint the final stretch alone. With about 250 meters remaining, he overtook American runner Yared Nuguse—himself the holder of the fourth-fastest mile time—and surged across the line, crossing every checkpoint faster than El Guerrouj had done a quarter-century earlier.

What makes Kerr's feat especially resonant is how it lifted those around him. Six other runners set personal bests in the same race, and Germany's Robert Farken ran a national record of 3:46.82. Speaking to BBC Sport moments after crossing the finish line, a humble Kerr reflected: "It's very overwhelming with the amount of hype [I created]... but I am surrounded by amazing people and was able to stay consistent." He becomes the seventh British athlete to hold the mile record and the first since Steve Cram in 1985.
quick_facts: Josh Kerr broke 27-year-old mile record at London Diamond League
New time: 3:42.66, beating Hicham El Guerrouj's 3:43.13 from 1999
Trained in Albuquerque, New Mexico under "Project 222"
Six other runners set personal bests in the same race
First British mile record holder since Steve Cram in 1985
